lines: Three thousand ducats, well.
fullText: Three thousand ducats, well.

lines: For three months, well.
fullText: For three months, well.

lines: Antonio shall become bound, well.
fullText: Antonio shall become bound, well.

lines: Three thousand ducats for three months,
and Antonio bound.
fullText: Three thousand ducats for three months, and Antonio bound.

lines: Antonio is a good man.
fullText: Antonio is a good man.

lines: Ho, no, no, no, no! My meaning in saying he
is a good man is to have you understand me that he
is sufficient. Yet his means are in supposition: he
hath an argosy bound to Tripolis, another to the
Indies. I understand, moreover, upon the Rialto,
he hath a third at Mexico, a fourth for England, and
other ventures he hath squandered abroad. But
ships are but boards, sailors but men; there be land
rats and water rats, water thieves and land
thieves--I mean pirates--and then there is the
peril of waters, winds, and rocks. The man is,
notwithstanding, sufficient. Three thousand ducats.
I think I may take his bond.
fullText: Ho, no, no, no, no! My meaning in saying he is a good man is to have you understand me that he is sufficient. Yet his means are in supposition: he hath an argosy bound to Tripolis, another to the Indies. I understand, moreover, upon the Rialto, he hath a third at Mexico, a fourth for England, and other ventures he hath squandered abroad. But ships are but boards, sailors but men; there be land rats and water rats, water thieves and land thieves--I mean pirates--and then there is the peril of waters, winds, and rocks. The man is, notwithstanding, sufficient. Three thousand ducats. I think I may take his bond.

lines: I will be assured I may. And that I may be
assured, I will bethink me. May I speak with
Antonio?
fullText: I will be assured I may. And that I may be assured, I will bethink me. May I speak with Antonio?

lines: Yes, to smell pork! To eat of the habitation
which your prophet the Nazarite conjured the
devil into! I will buy with you, sell with you, talk
with you, walk with you, and so following; but I
will not eat with you, drink with you, nor pray with
you.--What news on the Rialto?--Who is he comes
here?
fullText: Yes, to smell pork! To eat of the habitation which your prophet the Nazarite conjured the devil into! I will buy with you, sell with you, talk with you, walk with you, and so following; but I will not eat with you, drink with you, nor pray with you.--What news on the Rialto?--Who is he comes here?

lines: How like a fawning publican he looks!
I hate him for he is a Christian,
But more for that in low simplicity
He lends out money gratis and brings down
The rate of usance here with us in Venice.
If I can catch him once upon the hip,
I will feed fat the ancient grudge I bear him.
He hates our sacred nation, and he rails,
Even there where merchants most do congregate,
On me, my bargains, and my well-won thrift,
Which he calls "interest." Cursed be my tribe
If I forgive him!
fullText: How like a fawning publican he looks! I hate him for he is a Christian, But more for that in low simplicity He lends out money gratis and brings down The rate of usance here with us in Venice. If I can catch him once upon the hip, I will feed fat the ancient grudge I bear him. He hates our sacred nation, and he rails, Even there where merchants most do congregate, On me, my bargains, and my well-won thrift, Which he calls "interest." Cursed be my tribe If I forgive him!

lines: I am debating of my present store,
And, by the near guess of my memory,
I cannot instantly raise up the gross
Of full three thousand ducats. What of that?
Tubal, a wealthy Hebrew of my tribe,
Will furnish me. But soft, how many months
Do you desire? Rest you fair, good
signior!
Your Worship was the last man in our mouths.
fullText: I am debating of my present store, And, by the near guess of my memory, I cannot instantly raise up the gross Of full three thousand ducats. What of that? Tubal, a wealthy Hebrew of my tribe, Will furnish me. But soft, how many months Do you desire? Rest you fair, good signior! Your Worship was the last man in our mouths.

lines: Ay, ay, three thousand
ducats.
fullText: Ay, ay, three thousand ducats.

lines: I had forgot--three months.
You told me so.--
Well then, your bond. And let me see--but hear
you:
Methoughts you said you neither lend nor borrow
Upon advantage.
fullText: I had forgot--three months. You told me so.-- Well then, your bond. And let me see--but hear you: Methoughts you said you neither lend nor borrow Upon advantage.

lines: When Jacob grazed his Uncle Laban's sheep--
This Jacob from our holy Abram was
(As his wise mother wrought in his behalf)
The third possessor; ay, he was the third--
fullText: When Jacob grazed his Uncle Laban's sheep-- This Jacob from our holy Abram was (As his wise mother wrought in his behalf) The third possessor; ay, he was the third--

lines: No, not take interest, not, as you would say,
Directly "interest." Mark what Jacob did.
When Laban and himself were compromised
That all the eanlings which were streaked and pied
Should fall as Jacob's hire, the ewes being rank
In end of autumn turned to the rams,
And when the work of generation was
Between these woolly breeders in the act,
The skillful shepherd pilled me certain wands,
And in the doing of the deed of kind
He stuck them up before the fulsome ewes,
Who then conceiving did in eaning time
Fall parti-colored lambs, and those were Jacob's.
This was a way to thrive, and he was blest;
And thrift is blessing if men steal it not.
fullText: No, not take interest, not, as you would say, Directly "interest." Mark what Jacob did. When Laban and himself were compromised That all the eanlings which were streaked and pied Should fall as Jacob's hire, the ewes being rank In end of autumn turned to the rams, And when the work of generation was Between these woolly breeders in the act, The skillful shepherd pilled me certain wands, And in the doing of the deed of kind He stuck them up before the fulsome ewes, Who then conceiving did in eaning time Fall parti-colored lambs, and those were Jacob's. This was a way to thrive, and he was blest; And thrift is blessing if men steal it not.

lines: I cannot tell; I make it breed as fast.
But note me, signior--
fullText: I cannot tell; I make it breed as fast. But note me, signior--

lines: Three thousand ducats. 'Tis a good round sum.
Three months from twelve, then let me see, the
rate--
fullText: Three thousand ducats. 'Tis a good round sum. Three months from twelve, then let me see, the rate--

lines: Signior Antonio, many a time and oft
In the Rialto you have rated me
About my moneys and my usances.
Still have I borne it with a patient shrug
(For suff'rance is the badge of all our tribe).
You call me misbeliever, cutthroat dog,
And spet upon my Jewish gaberdine,
And all for use of that which is mine own.
Well then, it now appears you need my help.
Go to, then. You come to me and you say
"Shylock, we would have moneys"--you say so,
You, that did void your rheum upon my beard,
And foot me as you spurn a stranger cur
Over your threshold. Moneys is your suit.
What should I say to you? Should I not say
"Hath a dog money? Is it possible
A cur can lend three thousand ducats?" Or
Shall I bend low, and in a bondman's key,
With bated breath and whisp'ring humbleness,
Say this: "Fair sir, you spet on me on Wednesday
last;
You spurned me such a day; another time
You called me 'dog'; and for these courtesies
I'll lend you thus much moneys"?
fullText: Signior Antonio, many a time and oft In the Rialto you have rated me About my moneys and my usances. Still have I borne it with a patient shrug (For suff'rance is the badge of all our tribe). You call me misbeliever, cutthroat dog, And spet upon my Jewish gaberdine, And all for use of that which is mine own. Well then, it now appears you need my help. Go to, then. You come to me and you say "Shylock, we would have moneys"--you say so, You, that did void your rheum upon my beard, And foot me as you spurn a stranger cur Over your threshold. Moneys is your suit. What should I say to you? Should I not say "Hath a dog money? Is it possible A cur can lend three thousand ducats?" Or Shall I bend low, and in a bondman's key, With bated breath and whisp'ring humbleness, Say this: "Fair sir, you spet on me on Wednesday last; You spurned me such a day; another time You called me 'dog'; and for these courtesies I'll lend you thus much moneys"?

lines: Why, look you how you storm!
I would be friends with you and have your love,
Forget the shames that you have stained me with,
Supply your present wants, and take no doit
Of usance for my moneys, and you'll not hear me!
This is kind I offer.
fullText: Why, look you how you storm! I would be friends with you and have your love, Forget the shames that you have stained me with, Supply your present wants, and take no doit Of usance for my moneys, and you'll not hear me! This is kind I offer.

lines: This kindness will I show.
Go with me to a notary, seal me there
Your single bond; and in a merry sport,
If you repay me not on such a day,
In such a place, such sum or sums as are
Expressed in the condition, let the forfeit
Be nominated for an equal pound
Of your fair flesh, to be cut off and taken
In what part of your body pleaseth me.
fullText: This kindness will I show. Go with me to a notary, seal me there Your single bond; and in a merry sport, If you repay me not on such a day, In such a place, such sum or sums as are Expressed in the condition, let the forfeit Be nominated for an equal pound Of your fair flesh, to be cut off and taken In what part of your body pleaseth me.

lines: O father Abram, what these Christians are,
Whose own hard dealings teaches them suspect
The thoughts of others! Pray you tell me this:
If he should break his day, what should I gain
By the exaction of the forfeiture?
A pound of man's flesh taken from a man
Is not so estimable, profitable neither,
As flesh of muttons, beefs, or goats. I say,
To buy his favor I extend this friendship.
If he will take it, so. If not, adieu;
And for my love I pray you wrong me not.
fullText: O father Abram, what these Christians are, Whose own hard dealings teaches them suspect The thoughts of others! Pray you tell me this: If he should break his day, what should I gain By the exaction of the forfeiture? A pound of man's flesh taken from a man Is not so estimable, profitable neither, As flesh of muttons, beefs, or goats. I say, To buy his favor I extend this friendship. If he will take it, so. If not, adieu; And for my love I pray you wrong me not.

lines: Then meet me forthwith at the notary's.
Give him direction for this merry bond,
And I will go and purse the ducats straight,
See to my house left in the fearful guard
Of an unthrifty knave, and presently
I'll be with you.
fullText: Then meet me forthwith at the notary's. Give him direction for this merry bond, And I will go and purse the ducats straight, See to my house left in the fearful guard Of an unthrifty knave, and presently I'll be with you.

lines: Well, thou shalt see, thy eyes shall be thy judge,
The difference of old Shylock and Bassanio.--
What, Jessica!--Thou shalt not gormandize
As thou hast done with me--what, Jessica!--
And sleep, and snore, and rend apparel out.--
Why, Jessica, I say!
fullText: Well, thou shalt see, thy eyes shall be thy judge, The difference of old Shylock and Bassanio.-- What, Jessica!--Thou shalt not gormandize As thou hast done with me--what, Jessica!-- And sleep, and snore, and rend apparel out.-- Why, Jessica, I say!

lines: Who bids thee call? I do not bid thee call.
fullText: Who bids thee call? I do not bid thee call.

lines: I am bid forth to supper, Jessica.
There are my keys.--But wherefore should I go?
I am not bid for love. They flatter me.
But yet I'll go in hate, to feed upon
The prodigal Christian.--Jessica, my girl,
Look to my house.--I am right loath to go.
There is some ill a-brewing towards my rest,
For I did dream of money bags tonight.
fullText: I am bid forth to supper, Jessica. There are my keys.--But wherefore should I go? I am not bid for love. They flatter me. But yet I'll go in hate, to feed upon The prodigal Christian.--Jessica, my girl, Look to my house.--I am right loath to go. There is some ill a-brewing towards my rest, For I did dream of money bags tonight.

lines: So do I his.
fullText: So do I his.

lines: What, are there masques? Hear you me, Jessica,
Lock up my doors, and when you hear the drum
And the vile squealing of the wry-necked fife,
Clamber not you up to the casements then,
Nor thrust your head into the public street
To gaze on Christian fools with varnished faces,
But stop my house's ears (I mean my casements).
Let not the sound of shallow fopp'ry enter
My sober house. By Jacob's staff I swear
I have no mind of feasting forth tonight.
But I will go.--Go you before me, sirrah.
Say I will come.
fullText: What, are there masques? Hear you me, Jessica, Lock up my doors, and when you hear the drum And the vile squealing of the wry-necked fife, Clamber not you up to the casements then, Nor thrust your head into the public street To gaze on Christian fools with varnished faces, But stop my house's ears (I mean my casements). Let not the sound of shallow fopp'ry enter My sober house. By Jacob's staff I swear I have no mind of feasting forth tonight. But I will go.--Go you before me, sirrah. Say I will come.

lines: What says that fool of Hagar's offspring, ha?
fullText: What says that fool of Hagar's offspring, ha?

lines: The patch is kind enough, but a huge feeder,
Snail-slow in profit, and he sleeps by day
More than the wildcat. Drones hive not with me,
Therefore I part with him, and part with him
To one that I would have him help to waste
His borrowed purse. Well, Jessica, go in.
Perhaps I will return immediately.
Do as I bid you. Shut doors after you.
Fast bind, fast find--
A proverb never stale in thrifty mind.
fullText: The patch is kind enough, but a huge feeder, Snail-slow in profit, and he sleeps by day More than the wildcat. Drones hive not with me, Therefore I part with him, and part with him To one that I would have him help to waste His borrowed purse. Well, Jessica, go in. Perhaps I will return immediately. Do as I bid you. Shut doors after you. Fast bind, fast find-- A proverb never stale in thrifty mind.

lines: You knew, none so well, none so well as you,
of my daughter's flight.
fullText: You knew, none so well, none so well as you, of my daughter's flight.

lines: She is damned for it.
fullText: She is damned for it.

lines: My own flesh and blood to rebel!
fullText: My own flesh and blood to rebel!

lines: I say my daughter is my flesh and my blood.
fullText: I say my daughter is my flesh and my blood.

lines: There I have another bad match! A bankrout,
a prodigal, who dare scarce show his head on
the Rialto, a beggar that was used to come so smug
upon the mart! Let him look to his bond. He was
wont to call me usurer; let him look to his bond. He
was wont to lend money for a Christian cur'sy; let
him look to his bond.
fullText: There I have another bad match! A bankrout, a prodigal, who dare scarce show his head on the Rialto, a beggar that was used to come so smug upon the mart! Let him look to his bond. He was wont to call me usurer; let him look to his bond. He was wont to lend money for a Christian cur'sy; let him look to his bond.

lines: To bait fish withal; if it will feed nothing else,
it will feed my revenge. He hath disgraced me and
hindered me half a million, laughed at my losses,
mocked at my gains, scorned my nation, thwarted
my bargains, cooled my friends, heated mine enemies--
and what's his reason? I am a Jew. Hath not
a Jew eyes? Hath not a Jew hands, organs, dimensions,
senses, affections, passions? Fed with the
same food, hurt with the same weapons, subject to
the same diseases, healed by the same means,
warmed and cooled by the same winter and summer
as a Christian is? If you prick us, do we not
bleed? If you tickle us, do we not laugh? If you
poison us, do we not die? And if you wrong us, shall
we not revenge? If we are like you in the rest, we will
resemble you in that. If a Jew wrong a Christian,
what is his humility? Revenge. If a Christian wrong
a Jew, what should his sufferance be by Christian
example? Why, revenge! The villainy you teach me I
will execute, and it shall go hard but I will better the
instruction.
fullText: To bait fish withal; if it will feed nothing else, it will feed my revenge. He hath disgraced me and hindered me half a million, laughed at my losses, mocked at my gains, scorned my nation, thwarted my bargains, cooled my friends, heated mine enemies-- and what's his reason? I am a Jew. Hath not a Jew eyes? Hath not a Jew hands, organs, dimensions, senses, affections, passions? Fed with the same food, hurt with the same weapons, subject to the same diseases, healed by the same means, warmed and cooled by the same winter and summer as a Christian is? If you prick us, do we not bleed? If you tickle us, do we not laugh? If you poison us, do we not die? And if you wrong us, shall we not revenge? If we are like you in the rest, we will resemble you in that. If a Jew wrong a Christian, what is his humility? Revenge. If a Christian wrong a Jew, what should his sufferance be by Christian example? Why, revenge! The villainy you teach me I will execute, and it shall go hard but I will better the instruction.

lines: How now, Tubal, what news from Genoa?
Hast thou found my daughter?
fullText: How now, Tubal, what news from Genoa? Hast thou found my daughter?

lines: Why, there, there, there, there! A diamond
gone cost me two thousand ducats in Frankfurt!
The curse never fell upon our nation till now, I
never felt it till now. Two thousand ducats in that,
and other precious, precious jewels! I would my
daughter were dead at my foot and the jewels in her
ear; would she were hearsed at my foot and the
ducats in her coffin. No news of them? Why so? And
I know not what's spent in the search! Why, thou
loss upon loss! The thief gone with so much, and so
much to find the thief, and no satisfaction, no
revenge, nor no ill luck stirring but what lights a' my
shoulders, no sighs but a' my breathing, no tears but
a' my shedding.
fullText: Why, there, there, there, there! A diamond gone cost me two thousand ducats in Frankfurt! The curse never fell upon our nation till now, I never felt it till now. Two thousand ducats in that, and other precious, precious jewels! I would my daughter were dead at my foot and the jewels in her ear; would she were hearsed at my foot and the ducats in her coffin. No news of them? Why so? And I know not what's spent in the search! Why, thou loss upon loss! The thief gone with so much, and so much to find the thief, and no satisfaction, no revenge, nor no ill luck stirring but what lights a' my shoulders, no sighs but a' my breathing, no tears but a' my shedding.

lines: What, what, what? Ill luck, ill luck?
fullText: What, what, what? Ill luck, ill luck?

lines: I thank God, I thank God! Is it true, is it true?
fullText: I thank God, I thank God! Is it true, is it true?

lines: I thank thee, good Tubal. Good news, good
news! Ha, ha, heard in Genoa--
fullText: I thank thee, good Tubal. Good news, good news! Ha, ha, heard in Genoa--

lines: Thou stick'st a dagger in me. I shall never
see my gold again. Fourscore ducats at a sitting,
fourscore ducats!
fullText: Thou stick'st a dagger in me. I shall never see my gold again. Fourscore ducats at a sitting, fourscore ducats!

lines: I am very glad of it. I'll plague him, I'll
torture him. I am glad of it.
fullText: I am very glad of it. I'll plague him, I'll torture him. I am glad of it.

lines: Out upon her! Thou torturest me, Tubal. It
was my turquoise! I had it of Leah when I was a
bachelor. I would not have given it for a wilderness
of monkeys.
fullText: Out upon her! Thou torturest me, Tubal. It was my turquoise! I had it of Leah when I was a bachelor. I would not have given it for a wilderness of monkeys.

lines: Nay, that's true, that's very true. Go, Tubal,
fee me an officer. Bespeak him a fortnight before. I
will have the heart of him if he forfeit, for were he
out of Venice I can make what merchandise I will.
Go, Tubal, and meet me at our synagogue. Go, good
Tubal, at our synagogue, Tubal.
fullText: Nay, that's true, that's very true. Go, Tubal, fee me an officer. Bespeak him a fortnight before. I will have the heart of him if he forfeit, for were he out of Venice I can make what merchandise I will. Go, Tubal, and meet me at our synagogue. Go, good Tubal, at our synagogue, Tubal.

lines: Jailer, look to him. Tell not me of mercy.
This is the fool that lent out money gratis.
Jailer, look to him.
fullText: Jailer, look to him. Tell not me of mercy. This is the fool that lent out money gratis. Jailer, look to him.

lines: I'll have my bond. Speak not against my bond.
I have sworn an oath that I will have my bond.
Thou call'dst me dog before thou hadst a cause,
But since I am a dog, beware my fangs.
The Duke shall grant me justice.--I do wonder,
Thou naughty jailer, that thou art so fond
To come abroad with him at his request.
fullText: I'll have my bond. Speak not against my bond. I have sworn an oath that I will have my bond. Thou call'dst me dog before thou hadst a cause, But since I am a dog, beware my fangs. The Duke shall grant me justice.--I do wonder, Thou naughty jailer, that thou art so fond To come abroad with him at his request.

lines: I'll have my bond. I will not hear thee speak.
I'll have my bond, and therefore speak no more.
I'll not be made a soft and dull-eyed fool,
To shake the head, relent, and sigh, and yield
To Christian intercessors. Follow not!
I'll have no speaking. I will have my bond.
fullText: I'll have my bond. I will not hear thee speak. I'll have my bond, and therefore speak no more. I'll not be made a soft and dull-eyed fool, To shake the head, relent, and sigh, and yield To Christian intercessors. Follow not! I'll have no speaking. I will have my bond.

lines: I have possessed your Grace of what I purpose,
And by our holy Sabbath have I sworn
To have the due and forfeit of my bond.
If you deny it, let the danger light
Upon your charter and your city's freedom!
You'll ask me why I rather choose to have
A weight of carrion flesh than to receive
Three thousand ducats. I'll not answer that,
But say it is my humor. Is it answered?
What if my house be troubled with a rat,
And I be pleased to give ten thousand ducats
To have it baned? What, are you answered yet?
Some men there are love not a gaping pig,
Some that are mad if they behold a cat,
And others, when the bagpipe sings i' th' nose,
Cannot contain their urine; for affection
Masters oft passion, sways it to the mood
Of what it likes or loathes. Now for your answer:
As there is no firm reason to be rendered
Why he cannot abide a gaping pig,
Why he a harmless necessary cat,
Why he a woolen bagpipe, but of force
Must yield to such inevitable shame
As to offend, himself being offended,
So can I give no reason, nor I will not,
More than a lodged hate and a certain loathing
I bear Antonio, that I follow thus
A losing suit against him. Are you answered?
fullText: I have possessed your Grace of what I purpose, And by our holy Sabbath have I sworn To have the due and forfeit of my bond. If you deny it, let the danger light Upon your charter and your city's freedom! You'll ask me why I rather choose to have A weight of carrion flesh than to receive Three thousand ducats. I'll not answer that, But say it is my humor. Is it answered? What if my house be troubled with a rat, And I be pleased to give ten thousand ducats To have it baned? What, are you answered yet? Some men there are love not a gaping pig, Some that are mad if they behold a cat, And others, when the bagpipe sings i' th' nose, Cannot contain their urine; for affection Masters oft passion, sways it to the mood Of what it likes or loathes. Now for your answer: As there is no firm reason to be rendered Why he cannot abide a gaping pig, Why he a harmless necessary cat, Why he a woolen bagpipe, but of force Must yield to such inevitable shame As to offend, himself being offended, So can I give no reason, nor I will not, More than a lodged hate and a certain loathing I bear Antonio, that I follow thus A losing suit against him. Are you answered?

lines: I am not bound to please thee with my answers.
fullText: I am not bound to please thee with my answers.

lines: Hates any man the thing he would not kill?
fullText: Hates any man the thing he would not kill?

lines: What, wouldst thou have a serpent sting thee twice?
fullText: What, wouldst thou have a serpent sting thee twice?

lines: If every ducat in six thousand ducats
Were in six parts, and every part a ducat,
I would not draw them. I would have my bond.
fullText: If every ducat in six thousand ducats Were in six parts, and every part a ducat, I would not draw them. I would have my bond.

lines: What judgment shall I dread, doing no wrong?
You have among you many a purchased slave,
Which, like your asses and your dogs and mules,
You use in abject and in slavish parts
Because you bought them. Shall I say to you
"Let them be free! Marry them to your heirs!
Why sweat they under burdens? Let their beds
Be made as soft as yours, and let their palates
Be seasoned with such viands"? You will answer
"The slaves are ours!" So do I answer you:
The pound of flesh which I demand of him
Is dearly bought; 'tis mine and I will have it.
If you deny me, fie upon your law:
There is no force in the decrees of Venice.
I stand for judgment. Answer: shall I have it?
fullText: What judgment shall I dread, doing no wrong? You have among you many a purchased slave, Which, like your asses and your dogs and mules, You use in abject and in slavish parts Because you bought them. Shall I say to you "Let them be free! Marry them to your heirs! Why sweat they under burdens? Let their beds Be made as soft as yours, and let their palates Be seasoned with such viands"? You will answer "The slaves are ours!" So do I answer you: The pound of flesh which I demand of him Is dearly bought; 'tis mine and I will have it. If you deny me, fie upon your law: There is no force in the decrees of Venice. I stand for judgment. Answer: shall I have it?

lines: To cut the forfeiture from that bankrout there.
fullText: To cut the forfeiture from that bankrout there.

lines: No, none that thou hast wit enough to make.
fullText: No, none that thou hast wit enough to make.

lines: Till thou canst rail the seal from off my bond,
Thou but offend'st thy lungs to speak so loud.
Repair thy wit, good youth, or it will fall
To cureless ruin. I stand here for law.
fullText: Till thou canst rail the seal from off my bond, Thou but offend'st thy lungs to speak so loud. Repair thy wit, good youth, or it will fall To cureless ruin. I stand here for law.

lines: Shylock is my name.
fullText: Shylock is my name.

lines: On what compulsion must I? Tell me that.
fullText: On what compulsion must I? Tell me that.

lines: My deeds upon my head! I crave the law,
The penalty and forfeit of my bond.
fullText: My deeds upon my head! I crave the law, The penalty and forfeit of my bond.

lines: A Daniel come to judgment! Yea, a Daniel.
O wise young judge, how I do honor thee!
fullText: A Daniel come to judgment! Yea, a Daniel. O wise young judge, how I do honor thee!

lines: Here 'tis, most reverend doctor, here it is.
fullText: Here 'tis, most reverend doctor, here it is.

lines: An oath, an oath, I have an oath in heaven!
Shall I lay perjury upon my soul?
No, not for Venice!
fullText: An oath, an oath, I have an oath in heaven! Shall I lay perjury upon my soul? No, not for Venice!

lines: When it is paid according to the tenor.
It doth appear you are a worthy judge;
You know the law; your exposition
Hath been most sound. I charge you by the law,
Whereof you are a well-deserving pillar,
Proceed to judgment. By my soul I swear
There is no power in the tongue of man
To alter me. I stay here on my bond.
fullText: When it is paid according to the tenor. It doth appear you are a worthy judge; You know the law; your exposition Hath been most sound. I charge you by the law, Whereof you are a well-deserving pillar, Proceed to judgment. By my soul I swear There is no power in the tongue of man To alter me. I stay here on my bond.

lines: O noble judge! O excellent young man!
fullText: O noble judge! O excellent young man!

lines: 'Tis very true. O wise and upright judge,
How much more elder art thou than thy looks!
fullText: 'Tis very true. O wise and upright judge, How much more elder art thou than thy looks!

lines: Ay, his breast!
So says the bond, doth it not, noble judge?
"Nearest his heart." Those are the very words.
fullText: Ay, his breast! So says the bond, doth it not, noble judge? "Nearest his heart." Those are the very words.

lines: I have them ready.
fullText: I have them ready.

lines: Is it so nominated in the bond?
fullText: Is it so nominated in the bond?

lines: I cannot find it. 'Tis not in the bond.
fullText: I cannot find it. 'Tis not in the bond.

lines: These be the Christian husbands! I have a
daughter--
Would any of the stock of Barabbas
Had been her husband, rather than a Christian!
We trifle time. I pray thee, pursue sentence.
fullText: These be the Christian husbands! I have a daughter-- Would any of the stock of Barabbas Had been her husband, rather than a Christian! We trifle time. I pray thee, pursue sentence.

lines: Most rightful judge!
fullText: Most rightful judge!

lines: Most learned judge! A sentence!--Come, prepare.
fullText: Most learned judge! A sentence!--Come, prepare.

lines: Is that the law?
fullText: Is that the law?

lines: I take this offer then. Pay the bond thrice
And let the Christian go.
fullText: I take this offer then. Pay the bond thrice And let the Christian go.

lines: Give me my principal and let me go.
fullText: Give me my principal and let me go.

lines: Shall I not have barely my principal?
fullText: Shall I not have barely my principal?

lines: Why, then, the devil give him good of it!
I'll stay no longer question.
fullText: Why, then, the devil give him good of it! I'll stay no longer question.

lines: Nay, take my life and all. Pardon not that.
You take my house when you do take the prop
That doth sustain my house; you take my life
When you do take the means whereby I live.
fullText: Nay, take my life and all. Pardon not that. You take my house when you do take the prop That doth sustain my house; you take my life When you do take the means whereby I live.

lines: I am content.
fullText: I am content.

lines: I pray you give me leave to go from hence.
I am not well. Send the deed after me
And I will sign it.
fullText: I pray you give me leave to go from hence. I am not well. Send the deed after me And I will sign it.
